metadata.title: Principles of Premarket Pathways for Combination Products

rules.rule_text: A combination product is a product comprised of two or more different types of medical products (a combination of a drug, device, and/or biological product with one another); the drugs, devices, and biological products included are referred to as constituent parts.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — What Are Combination Products and How Are Their Center Assignments Determined?
rules.verbatim_quote: As set forth in section 503(g) of the FD&C Act and 21 CFR part 3, a combination product is a product comprised of two or more different types of medical products (i.e., a combination of a drug, device, and/or biological product with one another). The drugs, devices, and biological products included in combination products are referred to as constituent parts of the combination product.

rules.rule_text: A combination product is assigned to a lead center based on the primary mode of action (PMOA) of the combination product; the PMOA is the single mode of action expected to make the greatest contribution to the overall intended therapeutic effects.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — What Are Combination Products and How Are Their Center Assignments Determined?
rules.verbatim_quote: A combination product is assigned to an Agency center that will have primary jurisdiction (i.e., the lead) for that product's regulation. Under section 503(g)(1), assignment of a combination product to a lead center is based on a determination of which constituent part provides the primary mode of action (PMOA) of the combination product.

rules.rule_text: If uncertain or in disagreement about center assignment or product classification, sponsors may submit a Request for Designation (RFD) to obtain a binding classification and/or assignment determination from FDA; the Office of Combination Products (OCP) makes the final determination.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — What Are Combination Products and How Are Their Center Assignments Determined?
rules.verbatim_quote: If you are uncertain or disagree with a center on product classification or which center is the lead for a particular product, you may submit a request for designation (RFD) to obtain a binding classification and/or assignment determination from FDA, or a Pre-RFD to obtain informal feedback relating to the classification and/or assignment of your product... the Office of Combination Products (OCP) makes the final determination with input from the relevant Agency components.
rules.exceptions: If OCP does not respond to an RFD within 60 days of filing, the classification or assignment proposed by the sponsor will become the final determination.
cross_references: FDA guidance How to Write a Request for Designation (RFD)
FDA guidance How to Prepare a Pre-Request for Designation (Pre-RFD)

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must identify their products as combination products in seeking Agency action on forms including Form FDA 1571 (IND), Form FDA 356h (NDA/ANDA/BLA), IND submissions, IDE applications, 510(k) submissions, PMA, and De Novo requests.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — Basics of Interacting With FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: under section 503(g)(8)(C)(v), sponsors are required to identify their products as combination products in seeking Agency action with respect to the product. It is important that combination products be identified on the appropriate form or document: Form FDA 1571, INVESTIGATIONAL NEW DRUG APPLICATION; Form FDA 356h, APPLICATION TO MARKET A NEW OR ABBREVIATED NEW DRUG OR BIOLOGIC FOR HUMAN USE; or in the cover letter of a pre-investigational new drug application (IND) submission, investigational device exemption (IDE) application, Q-submission, 510(k) submission, premarket approval application (PMA), and/or request for classification submitted under section 513(f)(2) of the FD&C Act (De Novo request).

rules.rule_text: FDA shall conduct the premarket review of any combination product under a single application, whenever appropriate; a sponsor may choose to submit separate applications for the different constituent parts unless FDA determines that a single application is necessary.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: Section 503(g)(1)(B) and 503(g)(6) of the FD&C Act provide that FDA 'shall conduct the premarket review of any combination product under a single application, whenever appropriate' and that a sponsor may choose to submit separate applications for the different constituent parts of a combination product unless FDA 'determines that a single application is necessary.'
rules.exceptions: Separate applications would generally be permissible for the constituent parts of a cross-labeled combination product.

rules.rule_text: The marketing application type submitted should generally coincide with the PMOA of the combination product: a PMA, De Novo, or 510(k) for a device-led product; an NDA or ANDA for a drug-led product; or a BLA for a biologic-led combination product.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: The marketing application type submitted should generally coincide with the PMOA of the combination product (a PMA, De Novo, or 510(k) for a device-led combination product; an NDA or ANDA for a drug-led combination product; or a BLA for a biologic-led combination product).
rules.exceptions: If the PMOA-based application type does not enable substantially similar evaluation for each constituent part, the combination product should typically be reviewed in a different application type.

rules.rule_text: A single application for a combination product must enable a substantially similar evaluation to that which would be applied to each constituent part if reviewed under separate applications.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: To appropriately ensure the safety and effectiveness of a combination product in a single application, such application should enable a substantially similar evaluation to that which would be applied to each constituent part if they were reviewed under separate applications (e.g., an ANDA or NDA for a drug and a PMA, De Novo, or 510(k) for a device), including consideration of data and information needed under the separate applications.

rules.rule_text: For a device-led combination product with a drug constituent part (NDA-type drug), nonclinical pharmacology, toxicology, clinical pharmacology data, and CMC information are among the types of information that would typically be necessary in the device application.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: for a device-led combination product reviewed in an appropriate device application that includes a drug constituent part (that would otherwise be reviewed in an NDA), nonclinical pharmacology and toxicology and clinical pharmacology (including pharmacokinetic) data and chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) information are among the types of information that would typically be necessary.

rules.rule_text: For a combination product with a biological product constituent part, information regarding identity of the biological product constituent part and indicating compliance with donor eligibility or lot release requirements would typically be necessary.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: for a combination product that is not biologic-led and includes a biological product constituent part, certain information, including regarding the identity of the biological product constituent part, and indicating compliance with donor eligibility or lot release requirements, where applicable, would typically be necessary.

rules.rule_text: For a combination product that is not device-led and includes a device constituent part, engineering, biocompatibility, performance data, and other design validation data would typically be necessary.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: Likewise, for a combination product that is not device-led and includes a device constituent part, engineering, biocompatibility, performance data, and other design validation data would typically be necessary.

rules.rule_text: PMA approval is required by FDA before devices that are class III can be legally marketed; PMA must contain sufficient valid scientific evidence to reasonably assure safety and effectiveness for its intended use(s).
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.1 — Premarket Approval Applications (PMA)
rules.verbatim_quote: PMA approval is required by FDA before devices that are class III can be legally marketed. PMA approval is based on a determination by FDA that the PMA contains sufficient valid scientific evidence to reasonably assure that the device or device-led combination product is safe and effective for its intended use(s).

rules.rule_text: PMA applications for device-led combination products must contain sufficient data to demonstrate the safety and effectiveness of the combination product as a whole, including data regarding all constituent parts, technical data, non-clinical laboratory studies, and clinical investigations.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.1 — Premarket Approval Applications (PMA)
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should ensure that PMA applications for device-led combination products contain sufficient data to demonstrate the safety and effectiveness of the combination product as a whole, including data regarding all constituent part(s). The PMA includes sections containing, among other things, technical data, non-clinical laboratory studies, and clinical investigations.

rules.rule_text: Devices of a new type not previously classified are automatically classified into class III by operation of section 513(f)(1) of the FD&C Act; they may be classified into class I or class II under the De Novo classification process.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.2 — De Novo Classification Requests
rules.verbatim_quote: Devices of a new type that FDA has not previously classified or reclassified based on the criteria in section 513(a)(1) of the FD&C Act are automatically classified into class III by operation of section 513(f)(1) of the FD&C Act, and may be classified into class I or class II under the De Novo classification process.

rules.rule_text: If a product receives De Novo classification as class II, it is granted marketing authorization subject to general controls and identified special controls; a failure to comply with special controls will cause the product to be class III and subject to PMA approval.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.2 — De Novo Classification Requests
rules.verbatim_quote: If the product is classified as class II, it is granted marketing authorization subject to general controls, as well as identified special controls which provide a reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness. Such a product may serve as a legally marketed (predicate) product for future 510(k) submissions. If the product cannot be classified as class I or II, the De Novo request is declined and the product remains in class III and subject to PMA approval.

rules.rule_text: Generally, a device-only predicate cannot be used for a 510(k) for a device-led combination product because the addition of a drug or biological product constituent part would likely result in a new intended use and/or constitute different technological characteristics raising different questions of safety and effectiveness.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3 — Premarket Notification 510(k) Submissions
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, a device that is not combined with a drug or biological product constituent part could not be successfully used as a predicate for a 510(k) for a device-led combination product. This is because the addition of the drug or biological product constituent part would likely result in a new intended use and/or constitute a different technological characteristic that raises different questions of safety and effectiveness as compared to the predicate.

rules.rule_text: The 510(k) review standard for substantial equivalence requires that a product has the same intended use as the predicate product and either has the same technological characteristics OR has different technological characteristics but does not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness and demonstrates it is as safe and effective as the predicate.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3 — Premarket Notification 510(k) Submissions
rules.verbatim_quote: A product is substantially equivalent to a predicate product if it: has the same intended use as the predicate product; and has the same technological characteristics as the predicate product; or has the same intended use as the predicate product; has different technological characteristics; and the information submitted to FDA, including appropriate clinical or scientific data if deemed necessary, demonstrates that the product: does not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness than the predicate product; and demonstrates that the product is as safe and effective as the predicate product.

rules.rule_text: A product with a new intended use or with different technological characteristics raising different questions of safety and effectiveness cannot be cleared in a 510(k) submission.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3 — Premarket Notification 510(k) Submissions
rules.verbatim_quote: The following products cannot be cleared in a 510(k) submission: Product with a new intended use as compared to the predicate product; Product with different technological characteristics than the predicate product if such differences raise different questions of safety and effectiveness than the predicate product.

rules.rule_text: An NDA is generally the appropriate pathway for drug-led combination products other than generic versions; an NDA for a drug-led combination product must contain a demonstration of safety and effectiveness for the conditions prescribed, recommended, or suggested in the proposed labeling.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1 — New Drug Application (NDA)
rules.verbatim_quote: An NDA is generally the appropriate pathway for drug-led combination products other than generic versions of already-approved drug-led combination products. An NDA for a drug-led combination product must contain, among other things, a demonstration of the safety and effectiveness of the product for the conditions prescribed, recommended, or suggested in the proposed labeling.

rules.rule_text: A 505(b)(2) application permits reliance on FDA's finding of safety and/or effectiveness for an approved drug product; the 505(b)(2) pathway should not be used to obtain approval of duplicates of existing drug-led combination products eligible for ANDA approval.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1 — New Drug Application (NDA)
rules.verbatim_quote: The 505(b)(2) pathway should not be used to obtain approval of duplicates of existing drug-led combination products that are eligible for approval under section 505(j) of the FD&C Act (see next section) (see 21 CFR 314.101(d)(9)).

rules.rule_text: An ANDA must include sufficient information to demonstrate that the proposed product is bioequivalent to the reference listed drug (RLD) and to ensure the product's identity, strength, quality, and purity; it must also include sufficient information to demonstrate the non-lead constituent part is compatible for use with the final drug formulation.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.2 — Abbreviated New Drug Application (ANDA)
rules.verbatim_quote: ANDA must also include sufficient information to demonstrate that the proposed product is bioequivalent to the RLD, and to ensure the product's identity, strength, quality, and purity. ANDAs for drug-led combination products should include sufficient information to demonstrate that the non-lead constituent part is compatible for use with the final formulation of the drug constituent part.

rules.rule_text: In assessing therapeutic equivalence of a proposed generic drug-device combination product, FDA considers whether the proposed generic product can be expected to have the same clinical effect and safety profile as the RLD when administered to patients under conditions specified in the labeling.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.2 — Abbreviated New Drug Application (ANDA)
rules.verbatim_quote: as a general matter, in assessing the therapeutic equivalence of a proposed generic drug-device combination product, FDA intends to consider whether the proposed generic product can be expected to have the same clinical effect and safety profile as the RLD when administered to patients under the conditions specified in the labeling.
rules.exceptions: Any differences identified between a proposed generic combination product and its RLD should be adequately analyzed, scientifically justified, and otherwise not preclude approval under an ANDA.

rules.rule_text: Biologic-led combination products are licensed through BLA pathways under section 351 of the PHS Act; a biological product must be shown to be safe, pure, and potent, and the manufacturing facility must meet standards to ensure the product continues to be safe, pure, and potent.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1 — Biologics License Applications (BLAs) Submitted Under Section 351(a)
rules.verbatim_quote: To be licensed, a biological product must be shown to be safe, pure, and potent, and the facility in which the biological product is manufactured, processed, packed, or held must meet standards designed to ensure that the biological product continues to be safe, pure, and potent.

rules.rule_text: Section 351(k) BLA licensure would not be possible if a design difference in a delivery device results in a clinically meaningful difference in safety, purity, and potency; a different route of administration; a new condition of use not previously approved; or otherwise fails to meet the standard for biosimilarity.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.2 — BLAs for Biosimilar and Interchangeable Biological Products Submitted Under Section 351(k)
rules.verbatim_quote: The Biosimilars Q&A Guidance also explains that licensure under section 351(k) would not be possible if design difference in a delivery device results in any of the following: A clinically meaningful difference between the proposed product and the reference product in terms of safety, purity, and potency; A different route of administration; A condition of use (e.g., indication, dosing regimen) for which the reference product has not been previously approved; or A proposed biosimilar product that otherwise does not meet the standard for biosimilarity.

rules.rule_text: For a device-led combination product reviewed in a device application containing an approved drug constituent part, reliance on FDA's finding of safety and/or effectiveness for the approved drug is permissible in the device application when scientifically appropriate, subject to the provisions of section 503(g)(5).
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: For a device-led, device-drug combination product containing an approved drug (as defined in section 503(g)(5)(B) of the FD&C Act), reliance on FDA's finding of safety and/or effectiveness for the approved drug is permissible in a device application, when scientifically appropriate, subject to the provisions of section 503(g)(5), as added by the Cures Act.
rules.exceptions: FDA should generally only require additional data and information as may be needed to address additional questions of safety or effectiveness raised by the proposed use or function of the device in the combination product.

rules.rule_text: Prior to filing separate marketing authorization submissions for cross-labeled combination products, all interactions with FDA should be through the lead center for these combination products.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — Basics of Interacting With FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to the filing of separate marketing authorization submissions, all interactions with FDA should be through the lead center for these combination products, regardless of the feedback being requested.

rules.rule_text: Agency communications regarding the review from the lead center are considered communications on behalf of all centers involved in the review; centers must coordinate prior to issuance of such communications.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — Basics of Interacting With FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: As provided in section 503(g)(8)(C)(iii), Agency communications regarding the review from the lead center are considered communications on behalf of all centers involved in the review, to the extent consistent with the provisions of law and requirements of all affected centers. Accordingly, centers are expected to coordinate as appropriate prior to issuance of such communications.

rules.rule_text: For a device-led combination product De Novo submission, if the sponsor has a right of reference to the drug sponsor's NDA data, FDA will consider this data in its review of the De Novo request.
rules.source_section: Annex — Analysis of Pathway Availability for Device-Led Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: In determining whether to grant a request for De Novo classification, because the sponsor in this example has a right of reference to the data in the drug sponsor's NDA, FDA would consider this data in its review of the De Novo request.

rules.rule_text: A device-led combination product with a drug constituent part having a new intended use compared to the predicate will be found not substantially equivalent (NSE) and cannot be cleared via 510(k); it would require a PMA or may be suitable for De Novo classification.
rules.source_section: Annex — Analysis of Pathway Availability for Device-Led Combination Products (Example 1)
rules.verbatim_quote: the addition of the proposed drug constituent part and the indication of preventing infection are not applicable to the predicate product. These changes raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, precluding a meaningful comparison with the predicate product. Therefore, these changes in indications for use of the product and of adding the constituent part would result in a new intended use, and the product would be found not substantially equivalent (NSE).

rules.rule_text: A combination product with a different active ingredient from a predicate would differ significantly in design and materials, likely raising different questions of safety and effectiveness and causing the product to be not substantially equivalent.
rules.source_section: Annex — Analysis of Pathway Availability for Device-Led Combination Products (Example 5)
rules.verbatim_quote: Even if the special controls do not specify a particular active ingredient, a product with a different active ingredient from a predicate would differ significantly in features such as design and materials, which would likely raise different questions of safety and effectiveness and cause the product to be NSE.

rules.rule_text: For a De Novo classified combination product with a new drug indication added to its labeling, the product would be found NSE compared to the original predicate and would require a new PMA or a new De Novo classification.
rules.source_section: Annex — Analysis of Pathway Availability for Device-Led Combination Products (Example 2)
rules.verbatim_quote: These changes raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, precluding a meaningful comparison with the predicate product. Therefore, these changes in indications for use of the product and of the constituent part would result in a new intended use, and the product would be found NSE. The proposed product would require an approved PMA before it could be legally marketed. Alternatively, the product may be suitable for a new De Novo classification.

rules.rule_text: For a combination product reviewed under a single application, FDA takes into account the questions and considerations reflected in statutory and regulatory provisions associated with each constituent part, including how constituent parts interact and interrelate.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: Further, in determining what is needed to demonstrate the safety and effectiveness of the combination product, FDA takes into account the questions and considerations, reflected in the statutory and regulatory provisions associated with each constituent part in its review of the combination product as a whole and its constituent parts. This includes how the constituent parts may interact and interrelate.

rules.rule_text: For a drug or device-led combination product including a biological product constituent part that is not licensed for the intended use, information demonstrating the applicant has knowledge and direct control over the manufacturing process for the biological product constituent part would typically be necessary in the marketing application.
rules.source_section: Section III — Basics of Premarket Regulation of Combination Products
rules.verbatim_quote: commercial scale process validation data, information sufficient to enable FDA to conduct pre-approval inspections of the proposed commercial product during manufacturing operations, and information demonstrating that the applicant has knowledge of and direct control over the manufacturing process for the biological product constituent part (rather than by reference to a drug or device master file) if the biological product constituent part is not licensed for that use, typically would be necessary to include in a marketing application to support a determination that the combination product is safe and effective.
rules.exceptions: Failure to provide this information may result in FDA refusing to file the marketing application.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors may request in writing the participation of OCP representatives in meetings regarding their combination products, or to have OCP otherwise engage on regulatory matters concerning the product.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — Basics of Interacting With FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: As provided in section 503(g)(8)(C)(v), sponsors may request in writing the participation of representatives of OCP in meetings regarding their products, or to have OCP otherwise engage on regulatory matters concerning the product.

rules.rule_text: An interchangeable biological product must show it is biosimilar to the reference product AND can be expected to produce the same clinical result in any given patient, and if administered more than once, the risk of alternating or switching is not greater than the risk of using the reference product without alternation or switch.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.2 — BLAs for Biosimilar and Interchangeable Biological Products Submitted Under Section 351(k)
rules.verbatim_quote: To meet the interchangeability standard, an applicant must show that its product 'is biosimilar to the reference product,' and must further show that the product 'can be expected to produce the same clinical result as the reference product in any given patient' and that, for a product that is administered more than once to an individual, 'the risk in terms of safety or diminished efficacy of alternating or switching between use of the [two products] is not greater than the risk of using the reference product without such alternation or switch.'

rules.rule_text: For 510(k) submissions of combination products where different technological characteristics of a product do not raise new safety and effectiveness questions compared to the predicate, FDA reviews performance data to determine if the differences pose a significant safety or effectiveness concern; demonstration of substantial equivalence and compliance with applicable special controls may be sufficient for marketing authorization.
rules.source_section: Annex — Analysis of Pathway Availability for Device-Led Combination Products (Example 3)
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA reviews performance data (e.g., bench, animal, and/or clinical) to determine whether such differences pose a significant safety or effectiveness concern for the new product. This information is necessary to demonstrate the new product is substantially equivalent to Product A and/or is compliant with the applicable special controls... a demonstration of substantial equivalence and compliance with the special controls could be sufficient to demonstrate the safety and effectiveness of the change to the drug constituent part for the previously granted intended use.
